data.title: Calix: Recent Pullback Drives Bullish Update
data.description:
Calix, Inc. (CALX) has seen its stock fall under some pressure after the company warned of potential margin pressure given the rising prices across the memory ecosystem. Of course, this is something investors should be cautious about, but the magnitude of the pullback seems to be overdone.

Calix is one of the leading providers of cloud and software solutions to broadband service providers, essentially enabling high-speed internet deliverability and the related managing services. The company also supports broadband partners who are looking to further expand into underserved rural areas, supporting fiber deployment projects.

The company has undergone a transition from a legacy telecom hardware vendor into a higher-margin, recurring-revenue platform business. Historically dependent on selling broadband access infrastructure, Calix has pivoted its focus towards an end-to-end provider of cloud software and managed services.

For investors, the prospect of stickier, recurring revenue is attractive, and this pivot has also pushed for a higher margin profile. As seen in the stock chart above, the stock price rapidly appreciated as the company demonstrated early signs of success during this model transition.

This past quarter was a bit of a challenge as gross margins are expected to come under pressure. However, I believe this is directly related to supply-chain challenges rather than internal operational difficulties.

My last published article on Calix was probably a little premature in calling the top, but the stock has essentially round-tripped back to the starting point. Thus, long-term investors should closely follow this pullback, as it presents a great buying opportunity.

Financial Update

During the company's recent Q1 results, the financial metrics were actually quite solid. Revenue grew 27% yoy to $280 million and was close to the high end of guidance. Gross margin—which has been the big indicator investors look out for—came in at a healthy 57.2%. Ultimately, this led to non-GAAP EPS of $0.40, at the high end of the previously provided guidance range.

Although the financial results came within the guidance range, the challenge has been around the trajectory of gross margins. With the company still on its model transition journey towards higher-margin subscription revenue, it is of the utmost importance for the trajectory of margins to remain positive. However, this has not been the case before, with gross margins declining sequentially over the past four quarters.

Management has discussed one of the major reasons behind this pressure.

The sequential and year-over-year decreases related primarily to the transition from our second-generation platform to our third-generation platform during which time we operated in a dual cloud environment to successfully support customer migrations. We substantially completed this transition in the first quarter with no material impacts expected from dual clouds on our future quarterly results.

However, guidance for Q2 includes a gross margin of 54.25-57.25%, which would represent another sequential decline compared to the Q1 margin of 57.2%. Now, this is where investing in a model transition story is tricky.

At face value, the negative trajectory is concerning, and investors have already started to question the true profitability profile of the new subscription revenue. Yes, there are external factors impacting this, including higher memory costs (input).

Our gross margin guidance for the second quarter of 2026 is between 54.25% and 57.25%. Reflecting the effects of higher memory component costs, the impact from surcharges and the customer and product mix. The decline in appliance gross margin is expected to be offset by improvement in software and service gross margin as the dual clog costs abate, and we optimize the current cloud environment. For the year, we expect our non-GAAP gross margin to decline between 50 and 150 basis points.

I believe over time, the cyclicality of memory prices will revert to the mean. But in the meantime, Calix will be on the hook for absorbing much of these costs, as only so much can be passed through to customers.

Just a few days after the company's earnings report, Calix hosted an investor day, including the unveiling of its target financial model. As seen above, the company expects non-GAAP gross margin to improve by +100-200bps per year starting in 2027, essentially acknowledging the ongoing headwind in 2026. With OpEx expected to grow materially slower than revenue, this should set up for very strong margin expansion and EPS growth over the coming years.

In my opinion, the company's margin target is achievable. For starters, memory prices have rapidly increased, with 80-90% price increases for DRAM and NAND already in 2026. The extreme price increase makes it incrementally challenging for Calix to pass through to customers. However, over a longer time horizon, even if prices stay high, Calix will be able to pass through some of these expenses.

Probably more important to the margin trajectory is the company's ongoing model transition to subscription revenue. This mix shift will naturally drive higher gross margins. Even if memory prices continue on this extraordinary increase, Calix's ability to drive more subscription revenue will inherently drive margins higher.

Right now, the focus is much more on whether or not the trajectory of gross margin can improve. I am a believer that it will, and as discussed more below, this could result in material EPS growth over the next few years.

Valuation

With Calix not 100% a software company, valuation should be looked at as some sort of earnings multiple. The model transition can make a pure revenue multiple less relevant and potentially distort what's going on under the hood.

With the stock trading at ~24.4x forward EPS, it is difficult to argue this is a cheap buying opportunity. Add in concerns around the near-term margins and input costs; the downside could be well below 20x forward earnings.

According to Yahoo Finance, consensus currently expects 2026 EPS of $1.78.

Using the company's target financial model from above, 15%+ revenue growth combined with outsized margin expansion could easily result in ~25%+ EPS CAGR through 2028. Using this back-of-the-envelope math, this would result in a 2028 EPS of ~$2.80. In a downside scenario, this would probably be closer to $2.25, and an upside scenario could be closer to $3.50.

Even when assuming ongoing multiple contraction, 20x a projected 2028 downside EPS of $2.25 would still be a $45 stock, implying a flat stock over the next few years. Right now, I believe the market is pricing in something much closer to this scenario rather than a bullish outcome.

In a bullish scenario and assuming the multiple stays at 24x, this would result in a stock price of ~$84, or nearly doubling over the next two years. Yes, this is highly optimistic, but in my opinion, this quick math shows a positive downside-upside scenario.

Risks

The biggest risk to my bullish thesis is that memory prices remain high and Calix's gross margins continue to compress. If the current trajectory of gross margin continues, investors would become very hesitant to buy any weakness, and shares are likely to fall much lower.

Additionally, the company is still correlated to hardware cycles, which are typically cyclical and volatile. Yes, Calix is pivoting to a more software subscription model, but this transition is still ongoing.

Finally, Calix competes against much larger incumbent vendors, including Ciena Corporation (CIEN), Extreme Networks, Inc. (EXTR), and Cisco Systems, Inc. (CSCO). These vendors may have more financial flexibility to absorb the higher input costs, which ultimately could lead to pricing pressure.

Final Thoughts

The recent pullback in shares appears to be somewhat warranted given the gross margin pressure, macro uncertainty, and general concern around how AI will impact the future demand. Investors are rightfully concerned about these factors, and the stock has pulled to levels last seen over a year ago. Much of this has more to do with investor sentiment (lower multiple) rather than operational performance (positive earnings).

However, the recent pullback seems a bit overdone, and management laid out a confident roadmap, including a multi-year target financial framework. If Calix can start to achieve positive gross margin progression and demonstrate its ability to navigate this complicated supply chain, the stock appears to have much more upside potential than downside risk.

During the quarter, allocations to multi-family housing detracted from relative performance. Conversely, the Fund's exposures to other revenue, industrial and hospitals bonds were favorable.

In terms of credit quality, AA-rated bonds detracted slightly, while BBB and A-rated bonds improved relative performance.

The overall municipal (muni) bond market, as measured by the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index, returned -0.18% for the first quarter of 2026. Annualised GDP grew by a lower-than-expected 0.7% in the fourth quarter, compared to the preliminary estimate's 1.4% and the 4.4% expansion recorded in the third quarter. The US Federal Reserve (Fed) held the federal funds target range at 3.50-3.75% amid an uncertain macroeconomic backdrop. The yield spread between two- and 10-year Treasuries decreased as the curve modestly flattened, beginning the quarter at 69 basis points (bps) and ending the quarter at 52 bps.

Activity

Over the quarter, we focused on maintaining the Fund's duration through investments in short-term bonds and variable-rate securities. We continued to find good relative value for income-focused investors in these instruments as the front end of the yield curve remains inverted. The allocation of capital helped to bolster the Fund's yield as well as provide stability in the net asset value (NAV).

Outlook & strategy

As we move into the second quarter of 2026, we maintain a constructive outlook for the muni bond market, supported by strong fundamentals and gradually improving technical factors. While the yield curve has normalized modestly from prior inversions, we remain cautious about meaningfully extending duration as the opportunity for yield pick-up through extension remains limited and policy uncertainty continues to cloud the rate outlook.

Muni credit fundamentals remain solid. Tax collections continue to track favorably on a year-over-year basis, balance sheets are generally healthy, and reserve levels remain elevated across many issuers. In this environment, we believe muni bonds are well-positioned to navigate evolving interest-rate dynamics with relative stability and attractive tax-efficient income, particularly in the front end.

On the technical front, the muni market absorbed a record US$580 billion of issuance in 2025, supported by strong fund flows and reinvestment demand. Fund flows exceeded US$50 billion for the year, helping to offset elevated supply. Issuance in 2026 is expected to remain robust, with forecasts ranging from US$600-650 billion, driven by refunding activity and ongoing infrastructure investment needs. Issuance in the first quarter of 2026 totaled US$129.6 billion, up 7% year over year. While supply remains elevated, we believe demand from income-oriented investors should continue to provide support, particularly at the front end of the curve.

Against this backdrop, we are emphasizing credit fundamentals while selectively adding lower-rated names to capture attractive yields. We continue to favor issuers and sectors positioned to outperform in a potential economic slowdown. Maintaining the Fund's conservative duration positioning while focusing on credit fundamentals should result in a more stable NAV throughout an uncertain and volatile market landscape. With a relatively shorter duration over time, we feel the Fund should be insulated from sharp movements in its NAV relative to its peers.

data.title: Virtus KAR Small-Mid Cap Growth Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Market Summary

During the first quarter of 2026, market leadership shifted as small-cap stocks outperformed large caps amid a rotation away from crowded trades by investors. The Russell 2000® small-cap benchmark surpassed the broader market, as measured by the S&P 500®, and growth stocks (particularly large caps) pulled back as value stocks surged, signaling a notable change in investor preferences. Non-U.S. equities outperformed U.S. equities for much of the quarter, though that trend reversed sharply in March as the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz weighed more heavily on energy-dependent economies outside the U.S. While the quarter ended with a sharp rally on hopes that hostilities might ease and the Strait of Hormuz could reopen, markets remained highly sensitive to geopolitical developments. Energy was the top-performing sector across market capitalizations, benefiting from higher oil prices and driving strong returns for value-oriented benchmarks.

Fund Performance

The Virtus KAR Small-Mid Cap Growth Fund returned -9.09% in the quarter, underperforming the Russell 2500™ Growth Index's -3.52% return. Stock selection in the industrials sector, combined with stock selection and an overweight position in the financials sector, detracted from performance. Stock selection and an underweight position in the healthcare sector contributed positively to performance, as did an underweight position in the consumer discretionary sector.

LeMaitre Vascular (LMAT) and Teledyne Technologies (TDY) were the largest contributors to performance during the quarter.

  • LeMaitre, a provider of vascular medical devices, implants, and services, reported strong results for year-end and issued guidance ahead of expectations, which was due in part to a price increase the company telegraphed to customers that was well received.
  • Teledyne provides enabling technologies to sense, transmit, and analyze information for industrial growth markets. Demand has been solid across all the company's business segments, and its defense, space, and energy segments have grown. We believe the company's prudent mergers and acquisitions activity and frugal cost management have led to improved margins as well.

nCino (NCNO) and Ryan Specialty Holdings (RYAN) were the largest detractors from performance during the quarter.

  • Financial software company nCino's share price was under pressure, like many other software companies, due to investor concern about the potential for future disruption caused by AI.
  • International specialty insurance firm Ryan Specialty's organic growth pace moderated due to industry-wide declines in commercial property premiums. Market fears of disintermediation risk from AI have also pushed insurance broker shares lower.

Portfolio Changes

Considering the Fund's prolonged period of underperformance, we undertook a comprehensive review of the portfolio and exited more positions than in a typical quarter. We recognize that several investments have not panned out despite what appeared to be strong competitive protections. Drug development company Certara (CERT) is a good example of this. We also recognize that the risk of disintermediation from generative AI is not insignificant.

We have reviewed our technology holdings through the lens of disruption, not simply considering whether these companies can defend their market positions, but how new entrants may impact pricing and thus the future earnings power of these companies. Recognizing that the cone of outcomes for some companies is incredibly wide, we exited several positions. In addition to Certara (noted above), we also sold software and technology business Tyler Technologies, global credit reporting agency TransUnion (TRU), and UK online property portal Rightmove (RTMVF).

We also acknowledge that the portfolio's return on equity has trended in a negative direction, and we established several new positions, seeking to improve some of the quality characteristics we believe are important to long-term performance. During the quarter, we purchased iRadimed (IRMD), a specialized medical device company focused exclusively on MRI-compatible infusion pumps and patient monitoring systems; Hinge Health (HNGE), a leading digital provider of physical therapy in a fragmented market; New York Times Company, one of the largest news organizations that benefits from a strong brand built on credibility; SharkNinja (SN), a leader in small home appliances with its two brands, Shark and Ninja; and Watts Water Technologies, a company that designs, manufactures, and markets one of the broadest branded plumbing products portfolios.

data.title: Strawberry Fields Would Be Compelling If Not For Related-Party Deals
data.description:
At first glance, Strawberry Fields REIT (STRW) checks a lot of boxes as a potentially solid dividend compounder.

  • Senior care REIT with strong secular tailwinds.
  • Strong AFFO per share growth over the last five years.
  • Low AFFO payout ratio under 50%, leaving ample retained cash for growth.
  • Founder-led by CEO & Chairman Moishe Gubin.
  • Top executives with an operator background.
  • Significant insider buying since the IPO in December 2024.

On top of that, STRW has performed well since its IPO, outperforming two of its skilled nursing facility REIT peers in total returns:

My top pick in the senior care real estate space is CareTrust REIT (CTRE), a well-managed and low-leveraged landlord with a growing senior housing segment.

Meanwhile, Omega Healthcare Investors (OHI) and Sabra Health Care REIT (SBRA) have also enjoyed solid performance over the last year and a half while coming a little short of STRW's total returns.

I would argue that if an investor owned only one senior care REIT, it should be CTRE.

But is STRW attractive enough to merit holding along with CTRE?

My conclusion is that it is not.

Although STRW is interesting and worthy of going on the watchlist, there are some drawbacks to STRW that keep me from buying, and I'll discuss those below.

Strawberry's Pros And Cons

Chairman & CEO Moishe Gubin and his business partner Michael Blisko, a board member of STRW, have been in the senior care business together for over 20 years. They began by acquiring and operating skilled nursing facilities in the Midwest (Indiana and Illinois), which they did for over a decade.

In 2015, they separated the real estate from the operations, and that PropCo became Strawberry Fields. Since then, the company has grown rapidly from 33 properties to 143 across 10 US states.

After its IPO in December 2024, STRW is an almost pure-play skilled nursing facility ("SNF") REIT with a few odd properties like specialty hospitals and assisted living in the mix.

These are all triple-net leased properties where the tenant pays all property taxes, insurance, and maintenance costs. The typical lease terms are a 10-year initial term plus two 5-year option periods. STRW also makes ample use of master leases with its 17 tenant-operators.

The standard acquisition is at a 10% initial yield with roughly 3% annual rent escalators (2.8%, to be exact) and initial rent coverage of 1.25x.

The portfolio remains concentrated in middle America, having spread from Indiana/Illinois south into Kentucky and Tennessee, west into Missouri and Arkansas, and stretching all the way down into Texas.

Indiana is STRW's largest state at 25% of base rent, followed by Kentucky at 18%, Illinois at 16%, Tennessee at 13%, and Missouri at 12%.

STRW's average SNF occupancy rate stood at 76.2% as of November 2025. That is lower than the national average, but STRW initially structures leases for a minimum rent coverage that accounts for projected occupancy.

That said, STRW's rent coverage, while adequate, is not in the upper tier compared to peers.

As you can see, CTRE's average rent coverage ratio is over 50% higher than that of STRW.

Some states have unique reimbursement practices that allow operators to maintain lower occupancy rates. For example, the state of Oklahoma seeks to maintain at least one nursing home for each county and, as such, has fairly low occupancy per facility, but the state compensates operators more per day in order to keep them afloat.

STRW collected 100% of contractual rent last year.

STRW's formula of raising capital, buying 10%-yielding properties, collecting rent, raising dividends, and repeating has been successful over the years.

In the last five years, STRW has achieved double-digit AFFO per share growth.

Meanwhile, the REIT pays out less than half of its AFFO as dividends, leaving about $40 million in annual retained cash available for investment.

That is a significant source of low-cost (or free, depending on who you ask) capital to fund STRW's $100-150 million annual acquisition target. The rest will most likely come from incremental debt, as STRW's cost of equity is quite high right now.

As one last point in STRW's favor, I would mention the persistent insider buying over the last year or so.

These open-market purchases have been made between $9.92 and $13.10, compared to the current stock price of $12.50 (as of April 27th).

As you can see, there are plenty of points in STRW's favor.

It is led by a passionate founder-CEO, Moishe Gubin. The acquisitive growth model based on triple-net leased properties is simple and repeatable. AFFO per share growth has been quite strong. And multiple insiders have been buying shares over the last year.

Why, then, is STRW the cheapest REIT in its peer group?

In fact, STRW is by far the cheapest REIT in its peer group, trading at less than half the AFFO multiple as industry leader CTRE despite STRW's strong growth.

Why is the market so sour on STRW?

I see four significant problems.

First, STRW's balance sheet is a mixed bag with a few major downsides.

Although a net debt to EBITDA of 5.7x is not particularly concerning, especially for a triple-net leased REIT with a solid rent collection record, the REIT has a very large wall of debt maturities this year.

These bonds, maturing in July and September, bear interest in the high-6% territory. They will likely be refinanced at a similar interest rate, given the REIT's lack of credit rating.

Also, at least as of February 2026, STRW lacked a credit facility, although management stated that they are working on securing one.

Second, and related to the prior point, STRW lacks a competitive advantage when it comes to the cost of capital. Aside from its HUD-backed loans bearing interest at 3.9%, STRW's debt varies from 5.7% to 9.1%.

And STRW currently trades at an AFFO yield over 10%.

Going forward, unless STRW can secure more low-cost HUD debt, the REIT's weighted average cost of capital will probably float between 5%-7%. If not for the $40 million of retained cash after dividends, that WACC would rise above 8%.

On the Q4 2025 earnings conference call, the CEO mentioned that he would be traveling to Israel soon to negotiate the refinancing of this year's bond maturities. Once these Israeli bonds are refinanced, the debt maturity schedule should be more balanced.

So we expect in the next 45 to 60 days to be able to have most of our debt cleaned up and pushed off to have almost equal maturities over the next 4 or 5 years. And so we're really happy about that. I've been pushing that for a while. We will have a bunch of availability under our line of credit once it's done to over $100 million.

That leads to the third significant problem, which is that a huge portion of STRW's debt is denominated in Israeli shekels while all of the REIT's contractual revenue is in USD. This leads to foreign exchange risk as the two currencies fluctuate in value.

That said, it sounds as though STRW's preference is to refinance the Israeli bonds with USD-denominated debt, which will reduce (but not entirely eliminate) forex risk.

The fourth and perhaps most important problem I see with STRW is that almost half of all revenue comes from related-party operators.

I do not like related party transactions. I do not like them in a box. I do not like them with a fox.

If I know the individuals involved well enough, then I may be confident that business relationships between the related parties are still in shareholders' favor. But in situations like this where I don't know the related parties, I can't be confident that STRW management's interests are perfectly aligned with mine as the shareholder.

In STRW's case, the primary related party is Infinity Healthcare Management, which is owned by Moishe Gubin and Michael Blisko. They founded the operating company in 2008 and ran it together until spinning off the PropCo in 2015. Thereafter, Gubin became CEO of the real estate arm while Blisko remained as CEO of Infinity.

Gubin is a board member of Infinity, while Blisko is a board member of STRW.

(By the way, Infinity recently settled 30 death and injury lawsuits related to resident incidents at its facilities, paying out over $4 million for these. And a jury last year hit Infinity with a $12 million penalty, forcing the facility to shut down. This highlights the risk of SNF operators, who can be hit with large, punitive damages eliminating rent coverage or even forcing facilities to close.)

Infinity accounts for just shy of 50% of STRW's base rent.

None of this is necessarily suspicious or unscrupulous. Businesses engage in related-party transactions all the time.

But usually, retail investors of a publicly traded company are not involved in those related-party relationships.

Despite significant insider buying at STW, I am simply not convinced that interests are totally aligned between management and minority retail shareholders.

For example, directors of for-profit businesses are supposed to act in the best interest of the shareholders. So what happens if (or when) the financial interests of STRW and Infinity diverge?

I'm not sure. And that uncertainty represents the biggest drawback of STRW as a prospective investment.

I would guess it is also the biggest reason why STRW's valuation is materially lower than that of its peers.

Bottom Line

STRW has plenty going for it, as stated above.

While researching, I really began to get excited about the stock as a dividend growth opportunity. But on closer inspection, that initial excitement dissipated.

The balance sheet issues are resolvable, and management does appear to be in the process of fixing some of them.

But the related-party relationship between Gubin's STRW and Blisko's Infinity is a dealbreaker for me. It could indicate insufficient alignment between minority shareholders and management. And given how sizable this relationship is, I doubt it will change anytime soon.

Meanwhile, CTRE does not have related-party transactions, boasts a far stronger balance sheet with investment-grade credit ratings, enjoys a far lower cost of capital, and has materially higher average rent coverage.

For those, like me, who already own CTRE, I do not see a compelling reason to own STRW as well.

data.title: Intel: Back On Top, But Maybe Not For Long
data.description:
Intel Corporation (INTC) just reported its first quarter 2026 results, and the numbers were better than expected. Revenue for the quarter came in at around $13.6 billion, which outpaced estimates by about 9.22%.

However, EPS (diluted) landed at a loss of $0.73, largely because Intel’s GAAP results were weighed down by non-cash charges rather than operating weakness.

One thing worth noting, though, is that Intel’s EPS was affected by a $3.9 billion non-cash write-down on Mobileye (MBLY), which is an autonomous driving company that Intel acquired back in 2017, so it wasn’t a direct cash hit to the business. Stripping out this non-cash charge actually shows a non-GAAP EPS of positive $0.29, which was up 123% compared to the same period last year.

The result was Intel's share price surging roughly 24% following the earnings print, largely driven by a 22% increase in data center sales, as well as better-than-expected AI-related demand that signals stronger CPU demand down the line.

For that, and everything else mentioned in this analysis, my Buy rating for Intel stays in place.

Where Intel's stock stands right now

At the time of publication, Intel stock trades just shy of $85 per share, after jumping over 20% in a single day following its Q1 '26 earnings results. That alone added roughly $80 billion to Intel's market cap, and also pushed the stock past the all-time high it set during the dot-com bubble back in 2000.

Zooming out a bit, Intel entered 2026 near $37 per share, and before the earnings were released, the stock experienced several setbacks along with the broader semiconductor market. There were concerns around spending levels, and more recently, the ongoing tensions between the U.S. and Iran, which led to a macro-wide fear that pushed institutional investors away from broader tech stocks, helping explain the tech sell-off we saw over the past several weeks.

Interestingly, Intel was one of those names that wasn’t severely hit by the macroeconomic selloff. In fact, even before the earnings report was out, Intel shares had been steadily climbing by over 78% on a year-to-date basis. With the post-earnings rally, the YTD performance is now north of 115%, while the 12-month return is over 314%. And since my previous Intel analysis, the stock is up about 85%, significantly outpacing the broader S&P 500 (SP500) over the same period.

That said, the tradeoff is a relatively rich valuation. In this case, the forward price-earnings estimates for Intel are around 76x for 2026, or more than 136% above the sector median. Nvidia (NVDA), on the other hand, is estimated to be around 24.97x over the same period, which is relatively lower compared to Intel’s multiples, and even the sector median.

Well, it might be an isolated case since Nvidia appears to be significantly undervalued. But the point is that with Intel at these levels, the market seems to be pricing in years of profit recovery that, on a GAAP basis, haven’t shown up yet.

But what exactly got INTC stock surging higher? It was driven by a string of tangible developments. For example, Intel signed a deal with Alphabet (GOOG/GOOGL) to supply Xeon server CPUs for workloads across Google Cloud. It was a leap forward because the market has largely focused on GPU deployment in this AI infrastructure buildout, while missing the CPU side, and Google committed to Intel CPUs, which challenges the GPU-only narrative head-on.

More importantly, on the production side, the Core Ultra Series 3 CPUs started shipping in PCs back in January, while the newest Xeon 6+ data center chips hit the market in March. Core Series 3 is built on Intel’s 18A manufacturing process, while Xeon 6 is based on Intel 3.

Going back, the point is that Intel’s continuous upward trajectory isn’t built on speculation but actual execution and customer commitments, and that brings us to the company’s actual fundamentals.

Digging deeper into Intel’s first quarter 2026 earnings report

For the first quarter of 2026, Intel’s revenue rose 7% to $13.6 billion, which also came in $1.4 billion above the midpoint of the company’s own guidance range. The point is, a beat of that size at a company this large was possible because the demand showed up in a place management wasn’t fully counting on, but Intel’s conservative guidance philosophy over the past year may have ended up underselling what was actually happening in the business.

But I digress.

Intel’s gross margins for the quarter came in at 39.4%, which is up 2.5 percentage points compared to the same period last year. The improvement might not look so dramatic on paper, but it’s worth remembering that the company has spent the better part of the past two years stuck in the mid-30s, so the fact that Intel is pushing toward a gross margin of 40% proves it’s finally moving in a better direction, and this was all driven by stronger pricing power, products that carry higher margins, and better yields on the 18A process node- management confirmed on the earnings call that 18A yields are now running ahead of internal projections.

The yield aspect also deserves extra attention. In this case, yield is the percentage of chips that come off Intel’s production line in working condition, and while Intel hasn’t disclosed a number to the public, it’s the single most important variable in understanding whether a new manufacturing process makes money or burns it. However, having actual yield percentages to compare would be more helpful in seeing the full picture.

The bottom-line picture, however, is still a little rough. Intel’s net loss on a GAAP basis increased by over 350% to $3.7 billion, or a loss of $0.73 per share. Well, that looks like a step backward, and on the income statement, it technically is. But almost all of that setback traces back to a single event, which was the $3.9 billion non-cash goodwill impairment on Mobileye.

For starters, Intel paid $15.3 billion for the autonomous driving company back in 2017, and Mobileye’s market cap has since fallen about 35% from its December levels, which forced a write-down. Intel had to admit, at least on paper that the Mobileye deal is no longer worth what it once was, but no cash actually left the business.

On top of that, there’s another $1.1 billion in mark-to-market losses tied to a U.S. government share arrangement, which brings us to roughly $5 billion in non-cash charges masking what was otherwise a relatively better operating quarter.

But of course, the smart money isn’t too fixated on the headline numbers, and Intel’s core chip business is improving, as you can see from its Data Center and Foundry segments, which explains why the stock has gone up significantly despite the EPS missing Wall Street estimates.

Intel's showstopper

Speaking of segments, the standout was the Data Center and AI segment, which generated $5.1 billion in revenue for the quarter, up 22% compared to the same period last year and 7% sequentially, with an operating profit of $1.5 billion and a 31% margin.

Arguably, the market spent the last two years treating AI infrastructure as a GPU-only opportunity, and Intel’s data center business was supposed to be the casualty of that shift, but instead, the segment delivered its strongest growth rate in years.

And, in fact, Intel's CEO confirmed during the call that the ratio of CPUs to accelerators in data centers is shifting back toward CPUs. The exact quote was:

Xeon server demand is seeing strong and sustained momentum. Customers are deploying server CPUs along accelerators in the ratio that is moving back towards CPU.

Well, this makes a lot of sense, especially considering where AI is heading. The industry is now moving past the phase of training massive AI models into AI inference and, in the foreseeable future, into agentic AI, which refers to systems that can be tasked to do real-world functions like running automated workflows. The point is that agentic AI workflows run on CPUs, and Intel is positioning its Xeon lineup exactly for that workload.

Another important segment is the Client Computing Group, which is Intel’s PC business. This segment brought in $7.7 billion, up 1% compared to last year’s period, but the composition underneath this segment is what caught my attention.

AI PC revenue rose 8% sequentially and now accounts for over 60% of the client CPU mix. In other words, Intel is selling fewer chips but is getting paid more per chip as the product line shifts toward the higher end.

Meanwhile, Intel Foundry is doing fairly well on revenue growth. Revenue rose 16% year-over-year to $5.4 billion, while up 20% sequentially. However, it’s also taking hits from external foundry revenue, or the chips that Intel manufactures for outside customers.

That external revenue declined about 22% sequentially to $174 million, and the segment reported an operating loss of $2.4 billion- I’ll discuss more about this later, as it also stands as one of the risks around Intel.

Now, moving forward, management has guided second quarter 2026 revenue of $13.8 to $14.8 billion, with a midpoint of $14.3 billion, while GAAP EPS is expected to land at $0.08, which means Intel might be on the doorstep of profitability as we speak.

What could push Intel higher?

Right now, I'm most excited about Intel’s custom ASIC business, and I don’t think enough investors are paying attention to it yet. ASICs are chips designed for a customer’s specific workload, which is exactly the opposite of buying off-the-shelf solutions.

During the earnings call, management confirmed that this business grew >30% sequentially and nearly doubled year-over-year, and that it has already crossed a $1 billion annualized run rate, emphasizing that the business is still early in its ramp.

What makes this so compelling is that when you look at what the biggest cloud companies are doing right now, all of them are moving away from general-purpose GPUs and CPUs and moving toward custom silicon that’s built specifically for workloads they actually run. To me, this could mean Intel is ahead of the curve.

Now, at a run rate above $1 billion, which nearly doubled year-over-year, even a more conservative growth pace of 50 to 70% annually would bring ASIC revenue to somewhere around $1.5 to $1.7 billion by the end of 2026, which seems fair given how young these engagements are, and the fact that contracts typically run 3 to 5 years.

If we carry that forward to 2027, we’d be looking at a potential $2.5 to $3 billion business, and this is a higher-quality revenue stream than standard CPU sales since custom silicon commands premium pricing.

Another interesting factor is the way the ASIC business pulls customers into Intel’s packaging operations, which is basically a process where multiple chips get bundled into a single package for better performance.

Indeed, Intel disclosed that demand for advanced packaging scaled from what was previously expected as hundreds of millions to billions of dollars per year.

The point is that each ASIC engagement helps deepen a customer’s ties to Intel’s manufacturing ecosystem, and those relationships compound over time because switching costs can be high. And more importantly, with this dynamic, Intel is uniquely positioned.

You see, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) can manufacture chips but doesn’t design them. On the other hand, Broadcom (AVGO) designs the chips but doesn’t have the fabs to manufacture them.

Intel, however, does both. And if you include advanced packaging and 18A processes (and eventually, 14A) into the list, Intel offers a full-stack solution that won’t be easy for a single competitor to replicate.

This is one of the most underappreciated aspects of Intel right now.

What could potentially weigh INTC stock down?

With all that being said, investors need to pump the brakes, at least for a moment. Because for everything Intel got right this quarter, the foundry business still remains questionable, and it’s one that no amount of revenue beats can gloss over.

Again, Intel Foundry lost $2.4 billion in the first quarter on $5.4 billion of revenue. That’s a negative 44% operating margin. Basically, the loss only narrowed by $72 million from the previous quarter, which is almost nothing when you’re spending cash at that rate.

Deep in Intel’s full-year 2025 earnings calls, we find this same segment piled up $10.3 billion in operating losses on $17.8 billion of revenue. And at that current pace, Intel Foundry is staring down another roughly $9.6 billion in operating losses for 2026. That might actually be the biggest driver behind Intel’s $3.7 billion net loss in this quarter, where revenue came in $1.4 billion above expectations.

Now, going back a few quarters, the whole point of this foundry is to win outside customers, or companies that pay Intel to manufacture semiconductors for them. However, the external foundry revenue for the quarter dropped 22% sequentially to $174 million, but considering the $5.4 billion revenue base and the $2.4 billion losses, that’s still a rather minuscule number relative to the investment being made.

Well, management noted that multiple customers are actively looking at the 14A process node and that early design commitments should start showing up in the second half of 2026, which is fairly encouraging, but to be realistic, the revenue from those commitments is at least years away from being large enough to flip the equation.

There’s also another concern that deserves attention. Specifically, the CFO flagged on the call that Intel is bracing for PC demand to soften in the second half of 2026, with full-year unit volumes expected to drop by a low double-digit percentage. This is important because the Client Computing Group segment still makes up about 57% of the total revenue.

So, theoretically, a significant slowdown in this segment would affect the margins and the overall income statement. That’s the balancing act investors need to consider owning Intel's right now.

What’s my verdict on Intel?

Intel is a very different company than it was 18 months ago, and the gap between where the business is going and where the market thought it would be is still wider than most of us can see. There are risks, for sure, and they are expensive, but the company’s trajectory is clear, and I would rather own a company spending aggressively to build something durable than one cutting its way to relevance.

With that, and everything else mentioned above, my Buy rating for Intel stays in place.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bank of Japan's Hawkish Hold Reflects High Geopolitical Uncertainty
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang, Senior Economist, South Korea and Japan and Chris Turner, Global Head of Markets and Regional Head of Research for UK & CEE

Apparently, there was heated debate over today’s decision

The Bank of Japan extended its pause at 0.75% — in line with market consensus, though against our call for a 25 bp hike. We had thought that recent data showing growing inflationary pressures, real interest rates remaining in deep negative territory, and solid “Shunto” wage-negotiation results might support BoJ action. Today’s results show that, although concerns about inflation grew, the majority of board members prefer a wait-and-see approach.

Still, the 3-to-6 split is the largest divide among board members during Ueda’s governorship. We assume the split votes indicate that board discussions should focus on the timing of the rate hike rather than the direction.

The extent of dissent was a surprise. Board members Takata (who dissented in March), Tamura, and Nakagawa dissented. While Takata and Tamura are known for their hawkish views, Nakagawa's stance was quite unexpected, as she is considered among the doves. Her term ends June 29 after the June 16 meeting, making a June rate hike more likely.

Sharp upward revision of inflation outlook

In its quarterly macro-outlook report today, the BoJ raised its inflation forecast quite sharply to 2.8% year-on-year for fiscal year 2026 (up from 1.9%) and 2.3% for FY27 (up from 2.0%). The FY28 estimate is 2.0%. During the press conference, Ueda stated that the BoJ’s base case scenario anticipates oil prices returning to $70. The upward revision seems much more dramatic considering this BoJ assumption. In terms of growth, the GDP outlook was revised downward, though less sharply than inflation. GDP growth is 0.5% (from 1.0%) for FY26 and 0.7% (from 0.8%) for FY27. The FY28 outlook is 0.8%. The negative impact is mostly concentrated in 2026, and growth is expected to return above potential. The new outlook suggests that oil supply disruptions will modestly affect growth but will have a more prolonged and larger impact on inflation.

Meanwhile, the Bank of Japan’s new CPI indicators – core CPI excluding institutional factors – showed that inflation rose to 2.5% YoY in March from 2.2% in February, while nationwide core inflation rose 1.7%. It also clearly shows that underlying inflation stays above 2% and firms up.

BoJ watch

Despite the quite hawkish signals we gathered from the meeting statement and quarterly outlook report, Ueda didn't deliver a hawkish signal to the market. We believe the situation in the Middle East is fluid, so he would prefer not to provide a specific timeline for the next rate decision. Instead, he opened the possibility of future hikes. Ueda repeated basic comments that the BoJ is in the middle of a process to raise the policy rate to a neutral level and that, if the economy doesn't undergo a significant slowdown, rate hikes are possible.

We expect inflation pressures to accelerate and broaden in coming months. Tokyo CPI inflation will be released on Friday. We expect it to rise to 1.7% YoY. The government's gasoline price cap is expected to limit the gasoline price growth, but other energy-related prices - airfare, travel, logistics, and manufactured goods prices - are likely to rise faster. A weak JPY and bi-annual price adjustment should push up prices. Meanwhile, we expect GDP to soften in 2Q and 3Q26, but the government’s fiscal support will provide a cushion, preventing the economy from falling into a contraction this year. The government has released its oil reserves and is set to release more in May while increasing commodity imports from outside the Middle East. Therefore, the economy is likely to grow close to its potential level. Given this context, we expect that overall macroeconomic factors will lead the BoJ to implement a total of 50 bps by the end of 2026. While developments in the Middle East remain a swing factor, we believe the BoJ will raise rates once in June and again in the fourth quarter.

BoJ can’t save the yen

USD/JPY has come a little lower on hawkish dissent at today’s BoJ meeting, but the case for lower levels remains unmade. If energy prices remain elevated—or climb further—the yen becomes increasingly exposed, given the perception that the BoJ is reluctant to raise interest rates. For currency investors, the key focus now is real interest rates and the extent to which central banks are prepared to shield their economies from inflation.

The BoJ was running an accommodative policy even before this Middle East crisis hit. Its go-slow on tightening only risks real rates falling deeper into negative territory.

In reality, a 25bp rate hike from the BoJ in June or July may not make too much difference to the yen. A policy rate of 1.00% would be offset by core inflation expected above 2% over the BoJ’s forecast horizon. At the same time, the BoJ continues to brace for the forthcoming terms-of-trade shock. So far, this energy shock has been far smaller than that seen in 2022. Yet the negative pressure on the yen and on the currencies of the big Asian energy importers will likely dominate this year – barring a very quick resolution in the Gulf.

Given our view of a steady dollar this quarter as energy prices stay high – and perhaps a slightly hawkish Federal Reserve tomorrow – it looks as though upside pressure on USD/JPY will remain near term.

The big question, then, is where the BoJ will intervene. Investors will remember the very effective FX intervention undertaken in the summer of 2024 ($100bn sold) that triggered a major short squeeze in the yen. The problem for Japanese authorities now is that speculative yen shorts are only half the size they were in 2024. This will question the effectiveness and timing of any forthcoming intervention.

With yields deeply negative, a deteriorating terms-of-trade backdrop, and policymakers seemingly hesitant to intervene, the yen continues to offer little appeal for investors. Even if one liked the Japanese equity story, the yen remains very cheap to hedge. As such, we increasingly think USD/JPY will challenge the 2024 high of 162. There’s an outside risk that authorities hold off on intervening until the 165 area for intervention to be effective.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: John Wiley & Sons: Strong Fundamentals, But Colleges Are In A Bad Way
data.description:
I stumbled across an interesting infographic on Reddit (pictured below), showing that the average cost of textbooks increased by over 900% since 1978, far outpacing the 265% CPI gains. Another source suggests that textbooks increased by 1401% since 1974. I knew textbooks had increased dramatically, but posting nearly 4X or more gains compared to the CPI took me off guard.

After seeing the above chart, I took a pretty long fall down the rabbit hole. As I tumbled, I started to realize that higher education may be in worse shape than I realized. An increasing number of young individuals are questioning the value of college degrees. Unemployment among recent college graduates is historically high and the situation on the ground may be worse than what's on paper, which could further lower the value of a university degree.

Meanwhile, the gig economy is likely masking some unemployment metrics as many recent grads turn to apps to scrape together cash to get by (thus not counting as unemployed). Low fertility rates have resulted in a looming demographic cliff. AI’s potential as both teacher and research assistant may disrupt the education sector and diminish the practical value of textbooks and other learning tools. For these reasons, I’m downgrading my rating to a hold.

I covered John Wiley & Sons (WLY) back in October 2025 and assigned it a buy rating. Since then, WLY posted ~14% in returns, easily beating out the S&P 500’s ~6% returns. My buy rating was fueled in part by potential AI revenue streams. Data is very valuable for AI developers and Wiley has tons of data that it should be able to monetize. I still like the angle here, although I do wonder how long tailwinds will last.

The following sections focus primarily on the United States but I believe we would observe similar patterns and developments in many other countries. Also, the United States makes up about half of all of Wiley’s revenue and thus will have an outsized impact.

Low Fertility Rates Mean Fewer Young Adults To Enroll

Not so long ago people worried that population growth would eventually overwhelm the Earth. These days, analysts fret over population decline. Without immigration, America’s population may start declining in the 2030s and already, the number of young adults is decreasing. Even if immigration props up the population, it’s a reasonably safe bet that many of the immigrants will be older and less inclined to attend university.

At the onset of the Great Recession, people started having fewer babies, and the fertility rate has declined ever since (see below). It’s believed that high school graduating classes peaked in 2025, and by 2030 classes may be shrinking by more than 3%. Quite a few universities have already been shutting down owing to various factors. Once the potential applicant starts shrinking, the headwinds will likely become much stronger.

All of this is important for Wiley because few students means fewer textbooks sold. Demographic issues are not the only challenge Wiley will face. Changes in student preferences regarding where to study could also have an impact.

High Prices And Tough Economy Could Scare Students Off

There has been a shift in Gen Z education habits and many are increasingly skeptical of the price and commitment. Enrollment at public 4-year universities managed modest growth of 1.4%, but both private for-profit fell 2.% and private non-profit slid 1.6%. Community colleges are actually the fastest-growing segment at 3%. The impact of these shifts is hard to deduce, but some considerations:

  • Community college and vocational students are likely more price sensitive. Many people in these programs are trying to save money and/or upgrade their skills on the cheap. Wiley will need to take that sensitivity into account as expensive textbooks and learning tools could drive students away.
  • At least in my time as a student, community colleges utilized fewer books on average. A lot of my classes had a single textbook. When I moved onto a 4-year university, the classes often piled several books on.
  • Shorter programs and hands-on trades training will likely generate less demand for books than a 4-year university program.
  • It’s also possible that we could see some pushback from universities themselves. High costs can drive students away. Reducing textbook costs is one way to make things more affordable, and thus universities may try to demand cheaper books.

I’m sure there are other important considerations but I suspect the shifting enrollment numbers will, on the whole, be a net negative for WLY.

Fundamentals and Valuation Considerations

Wiley divides its business into two units, “research” and “learning.” The most recently reported results for both are below. The impact of declining enrollment should have a bigger impact on learning. Fortunately for Wiley, the research segment accounts for a larger share of the revenue.

Wiley’s revenues (YOY) shrank nearly 2%, but the GAAP PE ratio (TTM) of 14.29 and non-GAAP of 10.45 are both attractive. Ditto for the gross margin at 74.13% and the 9.24% net income margin is solid. All in all, I still like WLY from a fundamental perspective. This is a major part of why I rate the company a hold, despite my worries, rather than a sell.

I also put together a reverse DCF calculation, with the hurdle rate set to 10% (a reasonable return for investors to shoot for) and the terminal rate set to 4% to mirror typical global GDP growth. The rate needed to justify WLY’s current share is actually negative, suggesting the company could be undervalued. All in all, on paper, WLY looks attractive. Yet stepping back to see the big picture, I'd want the environment to improve.

Why I Could Be Wrong

The increasing enrollment in community colleges and vocational training may benefit Wiley more than I expect. Also, the shift towards vocational training may be temporary. It’s possible that international sales provide enough tailwinds to grow revenue and profits as well. I do believe AI is a great tailwind, but even still, I may be underestimating it.

Key Take-Away: Good Fundamentals And AI Growth, But Mind The Risks

Wiley is in pretty good shape in terms of fundamentals, although the revenue contraction is a serious red flag. The valuation metrics and profitability are solid, yet I do believe there will be sustained headwinds given the evolving education sector. For right now, I’ll watch and hold but if I conclude my worries over higher education are misplaced, I may once again rate WLY a buy.

data.title: Klaviyo: Lukewarm About This B2C CRM Software Stock Ahead Of Earnings
data.description:
Introduction

Klaviyo Inc. (KVYO), is a mid-cap stock from the broad application software universe, that is mainly noted for its CRM [Customer Relationship Management] offerings for B2C (Business to consumer) brands. Klaviyo, which has been in existence for 14 years now (during which it has serviced around 193K consumer businesses, in over 100 countries, mainly in areas of marketing automation), and began trading on the bourses less than 3 years ago (in September 2023), hasn't quite managed to satisfy its shareholder base during its time as a publicly-listed entity. To elaborate, in an era, where KVYO's software peers have notched positive returns of over 21%, and its mid-cap peers have surged by almost 50%, KVYO has lost ground by over 35% since it got listed!

I suspect the KVYO stock's next meaningful thrust will be keenly dictated by the events due to take place on the 5th of May 2026, when Klaviyo will release its Q1-26 earnings (post market hours).

What are the important metrics that investors should keep an eye on at the event, and how is its valuation and technical backdrop ahead of the key event?

Upcoming Earnings- Key Themes To Watch

With regards to the headlines, the two key numbers to focus on, are an expected revenue of $348.6M, and an adjusted EPS of $0.2. I'd like to think that the probability of KVYO falling short of those expectations are quite low, as historically, the company has "always" managed to trumped estimates with an average topline beat of 4.4% per quarter.

Another important consideration for investors is that, Q1 trends for KVYO, tend not to be as strong as what is seen as Q4, where the company typically benefits from customers (mainly retailers and those involved in eCommerce) engaging in more marketing campaigns and higher marketing spend related to the holiday season. In addition to that, also take note of the fact that KVYO has already been experiencing a slowing pace of annual topline growth over time, with the run rate dipping below 30% for the first time last quarter since the company got listed. In Q1-26, a slowing down of the run rate will almost certainly be seen, with management previously guiding to a range of 23.5%-25%, back in early-February.

What I also find dispiriting to note is that this growth slowdown won't end with Q1, but is likely to persist over the next 7 quarters at the very least, with topline growth expected to come in at less than 18% by the time we get to Q4-27. Even if you don't want to attach too much of currency to consensus expectations, note that the C-suite of KVYO have also only guided to annual topline growth of 22% for the whole of FY26, which would certainly imply a much slower Q2, Q3, and Q4 than the likely run rate of Q1.

For a growth company like KVYO to get hamstrung by a slowing pace of top line at this juncture is not ideal, even if it is better positioned than some of the other SaaS-based entities (particularly those that bill on the basis of seats) to weather the AI storm.

In recent years, KVYO's addressable market had seen a meaningful spike by 2.35x to $160B, as the company decided to transition beyond just offering marketing automation capabilities, to a more unified B2C platform encompassing marketing, servicing, data, and analytics. However, in H2 last year, they took yet another leap forward, as their enhanced B2C platform received another fillip with the incorporation of Marketing Agent and Customer Agent, functionalities that will leverage LLMs (large language models), and generative AI to help provide a more autonomous future for B2C companies across the world. KVYO is particularly well poised to make better use of its agents, as it already has a foundational database layer that has been built over the years (through the interaction of its retail and e-Commerce clients with their customers)

As KVYO doubles-down its efforts on the autonomous CRM path, I would also expect to see this attract greater traction from the mid-market and enterprise client category (customer categories which were previously underserved by KVYO in favor of entrepreneurs and small and medium businesses), which has been seen as a strategic priority in recent years. To get a sense of this, investors should see if customers with over $50K in ARR are still growing at well over twice the pace of the overall customer base (which has been growing at 16-17% annually for multiple quarters now).

Internal cash flow generation will also likely be a key area of focus, as KVYO does not carry any financial debt on its balance sheet (although it does have some lease liabilities), and will need to effectively fund a new $0.5B share buyback program announced last month (equating to 8.5% of the market-cap). While KVYO's annualized free cash flow margin (the level of free cash it generates for every $ of revenue) is now back over its long-term average of around 15%, I would expect this to dip in Q1 as payments linked to their employee cash bonus programs usually take place during this quarter.

Valuations- Not The Most Compelling CRM Software Pick

To get a more well-rounded sense of KVYO's valuation picture, I've measured it against other software stocks that are well-positioned within the CRM category. In that regard, I've also considered Braze (BRZE), Salesforce (CRM), Hubspot (HUBS), and Intuit (INTU).

Now, from an earnings perspective, what we can see is that KVYO is now priced at a forward multiple of around 24x, which is not particularly cheap, when there are three other alternatives priced at multiples of 13-18x. As a whole, KVYO is basically priced at a premium to this peer set average (of less than 22x).

Note that BRZE is something of an outlier on the earnings valuation front, trading at almost 37x, but I feel this can be comfortably justified as it is set to deliver outstanding forward earnings growth of 67%. Now, while KVYO offers decent enough forward earnings of 24%, it still lags the peer set average by around 400bps.

Then, even if we shift the lens from earnings to cash flow (which may be seen as preferable to a fair few, given that cash flow can't be manipulated by accounting effects, unlike earnings), KVYO's annualized free cash flow at the current market-cap doesn't look very compelling at only around 3.3%. In contrast, KVYO's peers offer better annualized FCFs at this respective market-caps (peer set average of 5%), suggesting that our stock-in-focus's valuations are not the most appealing.

Bearish Conditions Appear To Have Abated, But Lack of Momentum Still Exists

Prima facie, it could be dicey to suggest bullish positioning on KVYO, when it is still trading well below its important 200DMA (over a -25% gap), and that metric is also still downward sloping, rather than flat, or upward sloping.

However, without getting too myopic about developments on KVYO's standalone daily chart, I also thought it would be pertinent to highlight certain silver linings elsewhere.

Firstly, consider that short-selling appetite has taken quite a strong beating over the past month. Total shares sold short, has declined by almost a third over the past month, while the float component that was short which was previously at double-digits, is now only at a not-too-troubling figure of around 7%.

One could also argue that mean-reversion chasing investors who move their capital within the software universe, may have an eye on KVYO at this juncture, as its relative strength ratio [vs other software peers] is perched at a near-30% discount to its long-term mean of 0.181x and could be due some upward revision going forward.

Also consider developments on KVYO's larger time frame chart- the weekly chart. Here we can see that the $24 levels which had served as a key support zone earlier, had given way earlier this year. However, the downtrend, which had kicked off in December last year, and went on till February this year, appears to have since transitioned to a phase of accumulation. Also what's key to note is that, earlier this month, the selling seen did not result in a re-test of the pivot low of $15.5, which also suggests waning bearish appetite. Looking ahead, the next key test will be to see if KVYO can reclaim its old support of $24 (the black line); a solid earnings delivery at the Q1 event could be key to facilitating that.

Closing Thoughts

As Klaviyo approaches its Q1 results (a little over a week to go), I initiate coverage on its stock with a hold rating. Q1 is likely to be seasonally weaker (both from a topline and cash flow perspective), and the pace of topline growth is expected to slow even in the quarters ahead. I appreciate KVYO's capabilities in the agentic AI era, and will be looking to see if this can galvanize the acquisition of high ARR clients which have been growing at over 2x the pace of its standard customer count growth for a while now. KVYO's valuation backdrop doesn't look the most tantalizing, and while I can also see that bearish conditions are on the wane, the lack of momentum dissuades one from turning bullish.

data.title: Lazard Emerging Markets Equity Portfolio Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Equity markets in the developing world, as measured by the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, slipped 0.2% in the first quarter. The months' long rally across equity markets in the developing world—fueled in part by global investors seeking alternatives to US risk assets—came to an abrupt end in March due to escalating tensions in the Middle East, where the US's and Israel's joint military campaign against Iran curbed risk appetites. News that Iran had imposed a near-total blockade of the Strait of Hormuz in response to the airstrikes concerned many investors, who feared that its effective closure might have dire consequences for the global economy given that one-fifth of the world's oil and natural gas and roughly one-third of the world's fertilizer that sustains global crops pass through the narrow waterway. Global stock and oil markets were volatile throughout March, as investors searched for any signs that a de-escalation of hostilities was on the horizon. The price of Brent crude oil, the global benchmark, ended March at US$118.45 per barrel, a 63% increase since the war in Iran began on 28 February, and the largest monthly jump in percentage terms on record.

Emerging Asia, which is heavily reliant on crude oil from the Middle East, was the worst-performing region in the period, falling 1.5%, due largely to worries that the oil shock will adversely impact the region's economic outlook. Nevertheless, stock markets in global technology hubs Korea and Taiwan both rose sharply, thanks to the demand upcycle for memory chips. China's stock market fell as a result of a sell-off of AI-linked stocks. India's stock market fell on concerns about the impact that surging oil prices will have on the country's economy. Indonesia's stock market tumbled on news that index provider MSCI was considering removing the country as a constituent of the MSCI Emerging Markets Index due to low liquidity and ownership concentrations, which, if carried out, would require exchange-traded funds (ETFs) to remove Indonesian company shares to match the index's composition and weighting.

Latin America was the best-performing region, rising 14.6%, thanks largely to the region's limited exposure to crude oil from the Middle East. Colombia's stock market climbed, thanks to its status as a net oil exporter. Brazil's stock market rose sharply on bullish sentiment about the country's economic outlook and expectations that the country's central bank would soon start a rate-cutting cycle. Peru's stock market gained in sympathy with rising commodity prices.

The region encompassing emerging Europe, the Middle East, and Africa (EMEA) also outperformed, despite moving sideways. South Africa's stock market fell in sympathy with the steep drop in gold prices in March and on concerns about the country's economic outlook stemming from the country's heavy dependence on crude oil from the Middle East. Saudi Arabia's stock market rose in sympathy with soaring crude oil prices.

Communication services, consumer discretionary, and real estate were the worst-performing sectors. Information technology, energy, and utilities were the best-performing sectors.

(Index returns were measured net of taxes and in US dollar terms.)

Portfolio Review

In the first quarter, the Lazard Emerging Markets Equity Portfolio rose in absolute terms and outperformed its benchmark, the MSCI Emerging Markets Index. (Excess return was measured net of fees and in US dollar terms.)

Helped

  • Stock selection in the energy and financials sectors boosted relative performance, as did stock selection in Korea and China.
  • Higher-than-benchmark exposure to the energy sector and to Brazil and Mexico contributed.
  • Lower-than-benchmark exposure to the communication services and consumer discretionary sectors and to Korea and India were beneficial to relative performance.
  • Shares of ASE Technology (ASX) (1.4% Portfolio weighting) traded higher after the Taiwan-based chip testing and packaging provider reported better-than-expected full-year 2025 results, highlighted by earnings that grew 25% from a year earlier. Shares were boosted further after the company issued an upbeat outlook as it gains market share in advanced packaging services.
  • Brazil-based Oil producers Petroleo Brasileiro (PBR) (Petrobras) (1.6% Portfolio weighting) and Petro Rio (1.5% Portfolio weighting) saw their stock prices appreciate after reporting strong operational performance in the fourth quarter. Shares received an additional boost in sympathy with the surge in the price of oil.
  • Shares of Weichai Power (WEICF) (0.7% Portfolio weighting) gained on bullish sentiment about the China-based diesel engine manufacturer's upside potential based on opportunities to provide power generation for artificial intelligence AI data centers.
  • Shares of SK Hynix (3.0% Portfolio weighting) rose after the Korea-based semiconductor manufacturer reported better-than-expected quarterly earnings, underscoring ongoing strength in the price of memory chips.

Hurt

  • Stock selection in the information technology sector undercut relative performance, as did stock selection in Taiwan.
  • Higher-than-benchmark exposure to the consumer staples and financials sectors and to South Africa detracted.
  • Lower-than-benchmark exposure to the information technology sector and to Saudi Arabia and China and India were detrimental to relative performance.
  • Shares of Wivynn Corp (1.6% Portfolio weighting) traded lower after the Taiwan-based cloud information technology (IT) infrastructure provider reported weaker-than-expected fourth-quarter gross margins. Shares were weighed down further after the company announced a larger-than-expected issuance of convertible bonds.
  • Shares of Infosys (INFY) (1.1% Portfolio weighting) and Tata Consultancy (1.2% Portfolio weighting) fell as part of a broader market sell-off of IT service provider stocks on concerns about the capabilities of Anthropic's Claude code, which writes and tests code quickly with natural language prompts.
  • Shares of UPL (0.7% Portfolio weighting) fell on profit-taking after a period of strong stock-price appreciation stemming from news that the India-based agricultural chemicals manufacturer was exploring an initial public offering of its seeds business.
  • Shares of Axis Bank (AXBKY) (0.9% Portfolio weighting) fell on profit-taking after a period of strong stock-price appreciation in the wake of the India-based lender reporting better-than-expected quarterly earnings supported by robust loan growth.
  • Shares of insurer Sanlam (SLMAF) (1.0% Portfolio weighting) fell as part of a broader sell-off of South Africa's stock market due to concerns that the surging price of crude oil will adversely impact the country's economic outlook. South Africa is heavily reliant on oil imported from the Middle East.

Outlook

Market fundamentals remain fluid based upon significant new and existing geopolitical and trade risks. Our expectations will vary based upon these issues and valuations, but, at this juncture, we remain reasonably constructive on long-term prospects for emerging markets, especially if global growth remains positive and balanced.
