data.title: Investing For Life: Why Your Portfolio Is Your Most Valuable Asset Ever
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

Cash flow is valuable. That's something that every investor understands, and as companies are reporting their earnings over the next few weeks, we will be pulling up the 10-Qs and turning to the statement of cash flows. Sure, the "EPS" and other headline numbers are important as well, but the cash flows are where we find out how strong a company truly is. High earnings are great, but earnings that are being converted into free cash flow are the most valuable.

Most investors understand this, and you'll often see analysts in earnings calls asking questions about cash flows and what the cash will be used for. A company with significant excess cash flow that can be redirected as needed is a substantially better investment than a company that has minimal cash flow or cash flows that are already claimed for essential payments like interest.

So, why do we talk about our portfolio differently? Your stock portfolio is the most important business in the market for you. Sure, "book value", the theoretical liquidation value that your portfolio is trading at, is an interesting factoid. You can borrow against it, which I wouldn't recommend, or you can sell a piece of it to get cash. Yet, odds are you aren't going to sell every stock in your portfolio today. The current liquidation value, for practical purposes, is a number you don't intend to realize. It's a number that changes every minute that the market is open, and you have no way of knowing what it will be in 5 minutes, let alone 5 years.

I treat my portfolio like every other business. It generates revenues, it has expenses, and it pays me a dividend. My portfolio is a company that is in the business of generating investment cash flow for me.

Today, I want to take a look at the primary reasons I invest for cash flow.

Reason #1: Get Paid Today

Perhaps one of the most overlooked values of dividends is the time value of money. Money today is more valuable than money tomorrow. We understand this intuitively. Say I offered to pay you $1,000 a year from now or $500 now and the rest in a year, which would you prefer?

An investment that provides cash flow throughout the investment is more valuable than one that doesn't provide cash flow. The reason is that the cash flow can be used. One way of calculating this is to look at the IRR, or "Internal Rate of Return."

There are many calculators available on the Internet. Here is a look at an example. Which is better:

  1. You invest $100,000 in a dividend-paying investment that pays 10% per year for four years and then cuts its dividend to 8% of the original capital before being sold for $80,000 in year 8. Your total realized return is $152,000 on a $100,000 investment.
  2. You invest $100,000 in an investment that provides zero cash flows and sell it in year 8 for $174,500.

The answer is that the two investments have essentially the same internal rate of return. Here is the dividend investment: Source

Here is the investment with no cash flow:

Despite the latter having a higher gross total return, the dividend investment has a similar internal return because money is being extracted each year. And money has value. It is fungible, and it can be used for a number of different things, including making new investments.

Reason #2: Realized Gains Decrease Risk

Dividends are small realized gains that you realize with each payment. It is tangible cash that is coming out of the company and going into your hand. You can then decide whether to put that money at risk or not.

Consider one of my holdings that is going through a big downswing right now. Here is Eagle Point Credit Company Inc (ECC) since January 2021, with no dividends reinvested:

Horrible, right? It's down 63%; that's a lot. On the cash flow side, ECC has produced over $10,000 in dividends over this period. More than 100% of the initial investment.

Materially more than the index produced. So when comparing investments, you are looking at one that returned over $10,000 in cash to you and one that hasn't. What if we compared them with an apples-to-apples comparison where we withdrew the same amount of money? Let's withdraw $1,800/year, adjusting for inflation.

Suddenly, the difference isn't that large. In fact, until January 2026, ECC was outperforming materially.

When you match cash flows so that the same amount of money is being withdrawn, suddenly the indices lose their luster. When looking at your returns, it is important to be aware of the difference in cash flows between investments and ensure that you are comparing apples to apples—investments that have similar cash flows.

If you bought ECC in January 2021 and withdrew the dividends, it is literally impossible for you to have a negative total return regardless of what happens. If you bought an S&P 500 ETF, it is very possible to have a negative total return if the stock market declines over 40%. Which is a lot, but it is hardly an unprecedented amount.

The great thing about dividend-paying investments is that you get the opportunity to reaffirm your acceptance of the risk with each dividend. You can reinvest in the same company or a different company or keep the cash. If at any point you think conditions are unfavorable or the price is too high, you can just stop reinvesting and collect dividends—each one reducing your exposure to the risks inherent in the investment.

Reason #3: Cash is Flexible

We alluded to it above: cash is fungible. You get cash in your account, and you can use it for any number of things. The Wall Street complex is insistent that you need to reinvest everything forever. This makes sense because most on Wall Street make money from AUM (assets under management). They want you to invest your money, leave it invested forever, pass it on to your kids, who will hopefully also leave it invested forever, and take out a minimal amount.

Well, if you don't intend on ever using the money, why do you care about your investment returns at all? Why are you wasting your time?

Most of us are investing because we have some current or future financial need or desire. The most common reason is to retire in comfort. Yet, so many retire and sit there with their investments tied up in stocks that produce nothing. They fear selling because they know the stock market can crash. They fear running out of money. So the nest egg sits there, untouched, or only a minimal amount is withdrawn because investors don't know how much is safe to withdraw.

It's your money; you worked hard to build it up—it's ok to spend some of it.

Dividends coming into your portfolio provide you with a defined revenue. You know how much is coming in, and it's recurring. You can then use that cash for whatever your needs are. You want to reinvest, great. Do you want to reinvest in the same investments? Add new investments? Take a greater risk? Make your portfolio more conservative? Take some cash out for personal spending?

The choice is yours.

Conclusion: Managing Your Cash Flow Like a Business

Interest and dividends are the revenues of your portfolio's business. Like any other business, they aren't set in stone. Some will be explicitly variable, such as Royce Small-Cap Trust (RVT), a CEF (closed-end fund) that adjusts its dividend quarterly based on changes in NAV (net asset value).

Some will provide lower dividends up front but will provide consistent growth. Realty Income (O), "The Monthly Dividend Company", is a REIT in our portfolio that is part of the Dividend Aristocrats.

Other investments will pay 100% of their cash flow and will see their dividends adjust relatively frequently. Annaly Capital Management, Inc. (NLY) is a mortgage REIT that has paid a quarterly dividend that has ranged from a peak of $3.00 to a low of $0.40.

Still others will pay a relatively flat amount and only change their dividend occasionally, like PIMCO Corporate & Income Opportunity Fund (PTY).

Your income will not always be consistent, but like a business, if you withdraw a portion of your cash flow and reinvest the rest, your portfolio will produce more income next year.

The result looks something like this. Here are these four holdings, from 2003 to 2026. Withdrawing $7,500 in year one and increasing withdrawals to match inflation. All excess dividends are reinvested:

Because the payout was conservative, the portfolio always produced more dividends than were withdrawn. Over time, the income grew, and it grew faster than inflation.

In fact, by 2025, the scheduled withdrawal of $13,435 was less than half of the dividends the portfolio produced:

This means that the investor can comfortably give themselves a raise.

Past results aren't a guarantee of the future. Dividends do get cut; they got cut in this sample portfolio. We recommend withdrawing a maximum of 75% of the cash flow your portfolio is producing to allow for reinvestment and to provide a cushion for when things go wrong.

However, you will know the day you withdraw cash that your portfolio produced it. You will know how close you are to the guidelines, and you can take action if something is entering into a danger zone. If you need to make changes, you can make them because cash flow gives you an objective measure to determine how your portfolio is performing.

Manage your portfolio like a business, and it will reward you like a business. That's The Income Method.
